perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party undertakes to keep the Information confidential and not to disclose it to any third parties and to avoid the risk of the information being disclosed to any third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party undertakes to try to keep the Information confidential and avoid the risk of undue disclosure to third parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'keep the Information confidential' to 'try to keep the Information confidential' and 'avoid the risk of the information being disclosed' to 'avoid the risk of undue disclosure' introduces ambiguity, weakening the obligation. 'Try' implies effort rather than a guarantee of confidentiality, and 'undue disclosure' is vague and open to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Replacing 'keep confidential' with 'try to keep confidential' suggests a lower standard of care than is typically required to demonstrate reasonable efforts under CUTSA, potentially weakening trade secret protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting CUTSA's requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original text states the Receiving Party 'undertakes to keep the Information confidential', implying a firm commitment. The modified text uses 'try to keep the Information confidential', which introduces ambiguity and suggests a lower standard of care. Additionally, changing 'avoid the risk of the information being disclosed' to 'avoid the risk of undue disclosure' further weakens the protection, as 'undue' is a subjective term. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Code, CIV 3426.1, defines 'Misappropriation' which includes 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the obligation to maintain secrecy, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation and may fail to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard implicitly required by trade secret law to prevent such breaches, as suggested by the Law Explanation. The change introduces ambiguity making the obligation difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party ag rees not to use the In formation in any other way than set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use the Information primarily in accordance with the general intent of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the clear prohibition of use outside the agreement's terms with a 'general intent' standard introduces ambiguity. 'Primarily' and 'general intent' are subjective and do not provide a clear standard for permissible use, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to deviate from the agreed-upon terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects against the misappropriation of trade secrets. Using the information outside the bounds of the original agreement is a potential misappropriation. By introducing a 'general intent' standard, the new text introduces vagueness that may create an opening for the information to be used in a way not intended by the disclosing party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity, weakening the protection against misappropriation of trade secrets. The original text clearly prohibits use outside the agreement's terms. The changed text, however, allows use 'primarily in accordance with the general intent,' which introduces a subjective standard. Snippet 1, defining 'Misappropriation' in CIV 3426.1(b), includes 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By introducing 'general intent', the change creates an opening for uses not originally intended, making it more difficult to prove misappropriation because the Receiving Party can argue their use aligns with their interpretation of the 'general intent' even if it falls outside the explicitly agreed upon terms.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall register all employees or others having gained insight into the Information. The register must be accessible to the Inventor/Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor to maintain a record of employees or others with access to the Information. Access to the record may be granted to the Inventor/Disclosing Party upon reasonable request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall register' to 'shall endeavor to maintain a record' weakens the obligation to create a comprehensive register. 'Endeavor' suggests an attempt rather than a strict requirement. Changing 'The register must be accessible' to 'Access to the record may be granted upon reasonable request' introduces discretion in providing access, which can impede the Disclosing Party's ability to monitor compliance. These changes introduce ambiguity on the level of compliance required.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, a trade secret owner must take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of their trade secrets. Impediments to the tracking of employees or others with knowledge of the information being tracked can create an unsafe situation wherein a trade secret is revealed, lost or misappropriated due to that lack of internal oversight. The changes made to this section may contradict those reasonable measures that are expected under CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall register' to 'shall endeavor to maintain a record' weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets. The original text created a clear and enforceable obligation to register all employees with access to the information. The modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion, potentially contradicting the 'reasonable measures' requirement under CUTSA, as trade secret owners are expected to take precautions to safeguard their trade secrets. The provided law snippet states that 'Improper means' includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the obligation to track who has access to the information, the changed text undermines the duty to maintain secrecy and therefore contradicts the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: At any time, the Inventor/Disclosing Party is entitled to be informed about how the Receiving Party keeps the Information conﬁdential in relation to third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Inventor/Disclosing Party may inquire about the Receiving Party's general confidentiality practices.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is entitled to be informed about how the Receiving Party keeps the Information confidential' to 'may inquire about the Receiving Party's general confidentiality practices' significantly reduces the Disclosing Party's right to specific information. 'May inquire' introduces discretion, and 'general confidentiality practices' is vague and may not provide adequate insight into the protection of the specific information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, the disclosing party is expected to take steps to protect their trade secrets. A restriction of information can reduce visibility into whether those steps are being taken and thus reduce the security of the information that has been provided under the agreement.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original text grants the Inventor/Disclosing Party the right to be informed about how their information is kept confidential. The changed text weakens this right, only allowing them to inquire about the Receiving Party's 'general confidentiality practices.' This creates a legal contradiction because the original contract ensured a level of transparency and control over the protection of their trade secrets, while the modified version introduces ambiguity and reduces the scope of information the disclosing party is entitled to. Snippet 1 defines misappropriation and improper means, implying an expectation for protecting trade secrets, which is undermined by the reduced transparency in the modified contract.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party is held liable to the Inventor/Disclosing Party for damages in the event of breach of this Agreement. The Receiving Party is also held liable in case of breach of the Agreement by third parties to whom the Receiving Party has handed over the Information wholly or partly.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may be liable to the Inventor/Disclosing Party for direct damages demonstrably caused by a material breach of this Agreement. The Receiving Party's liability for third-party breaches shall be subject to reasonable limitations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is held liable' to 'may be liable for direct damages demonstrably caused by a material breach' introduces ambiguity and limitations. 'May be liable' suggests uncertainty, 'direct damages' limits the scope of potential damages, and 'demonstrably caused' adds a burden of proof. 'Reasonable limitations' on third-party liability is vague and undefined, potentially reducing the Receiving Party's responsibility for their actions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, the law allows for the recovery of damages for the misappropriation of a trade secret. Those damages may involve direct damages, but can also involve unjust enrichment and exemplary damages under certain circumstances. The law also allows for injunctive relief. The new ambiguous edits potentially contradict aspects of these damages and potential remedies, thus limiting the means by which the disclosing party is made whole.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.3. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.3. (a) A complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation. A complainant also may recover for the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing damages for actual loss. (b) If neither damages nor unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation are provable, the court may order payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subdivision (a) or (b). (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract language states the Receiving Party 'is held liable,' which implies a clear obligation. The changed text introduces 'may be liable,' weakening the obligation and creating ambiguity. This contradicts the spirit of CUTSA (as described in the Law Explanation) which seeks to provide remedies to the disclosing party, including direct damages, unjust enrichment, and exemplary damages. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 3426.3(a), specifies that 'A complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation,' suggesting a right to recover damages for actual loss. By introducing 'may be liable' and limiting liability to 'direct damages demonstrably caused by a material breach,' the changed text potentially reduces the scope of recoverable damages and increases the burden of proof on the Inventor/Disclosing Party, creating a legal contradiction by undermining the potential for full recovery as intended by law.
